---
title: "*Add Friction and Work to Bernoulli? Here’s What Really Happens!* (The Mechanical Energy Balance) Reference Page"
author: "DOFPro group"
format: html
html-math-method: mathjax
---
::: {.callout-important title="Still Under Construction!"}
This web page is just a bare outline. The complete details have not been filled in yet.
:::
# Intro to *Add Friction and Work to Bernoulli? Here’s What Really Happens!*

Main title explanation

## Add Friction and Work to Bernoulli? Here’s What Really Happens!

Video 1 explanation

<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/Da48oZb9oNw?si=8VfEWxpZ1-ceS518" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
[Visuals](../../NotYetHere.qmd)

# Examples and Definitions

## Examples Links

## Definitions

[Friction Loss](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydraulic_head#Head_loss), $\hat{F}$
: The energy due to friction within a flowing fluid and between the flowing fluid and its container, such as pipe walls.

[Moody Chart](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moody_chart)
: Plots the Moody or Fanning friction factor as a function of the Reynolds number and the relative roughness for fully developed flow in a conduit or pipe.

[Moody Friction Factor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Darcy_friction_factor_formulae), $f$
: An empirical value used to determine the friction loss, often determined from a Moody chart.

[Fanning Friction Factor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fanning_friction_factor), $f$
: An empirical value used to determine the friction loss, often determined from a Moody chart. The Fanning friction factor is the Moody friction factor over 4

Mechanical Energy Balance
: A form of the 1^st^ Law for constant-density fluids with only frictional heating.

[Bernoulli Equation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bernoulli%27s_principle)
: The mechanical energy balance with the friction and work terms removed.

[Reynolds Number](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reynolds_number)
: A [dimensionless number](../../basics/processdata/DimensionlessNumbersVideoInfoWebPage.qmd) comparing inertial forces to viscous forces in fluid flow. For a pipe or tube, it's calculated as $Re = \rho u D/\mu$

# Other Links and Videos

## Other Links

## Previous and Following Videos

### The video previous to the previous video (i.e., two videos back)

**Using DWSIM to Create Binary Phase Diagrams**<br/>*DWSIM, Binary Phase Diagrams*<br/>[Video](https://youtu.be/5NUchIg8q7E), [Info Page](../../balances/material/DWSIMBinaryPhaseVideoInfoWebPage.qmd)

### The previous video (i.e., one video back)

**Just Try to Break This Law! I Dare You!**<br/>*Intro to 1st Law*<br/>[Video](https://youtu.be/ZHWT9Jw7fzY), [Info Page](../../balances/energy/Intro1stLawVideoInfoWebPage.qmd), [Visuals](../../balances/energy/89-Intro1stLawVisuals.qmd)

### The next video (i.e., one video forward)

**Steam: Get the Quality Right AFTER You Table It! Part 1**<br/>*The Basics of Steam Tables*<br/>[Video](https://youtu.be/8tH3gXz_M8A), [Info Page](../../balances/energy/SteamTablesVideoInfoWebPage.qmd), [Visuals](../../balances/energy/91-ThermodynamicPropertyTables1Visuals.qmd)

### The next after next video (i.e. two videos forward)

**Steam: Get the Quality Right AFTER You Table It! Part 2**<br/>*Steam Table Examples*<br/>[Video](https://youtu.be/yRf09eSRM1A), [Info Page](../../balances/energy/SteamTablesVideoInfoWebPage.qmd), [Visuals](../../balances/energy/92-ThermodynamicPropertyTables2Visuals.qmd)
